name: Apogee Tasmania
description: Apogee is two hectares. That scale is not incidental — it is the argument. Andrew Pirie, who once managed Tasmanian vineyards running to over 400 hectares, deliberately returned to something the size of a Champagne grower's holding to test a single proposition: that perfect vineyard management has a hard upper limit, and two hectares is close to it.

The focus here is sparkling wine, made from Chardonnay, Pinot Noir, and Pinot Meunier using traditional method. The flagship Deluxe Vintage Brut runs roughly 50% Chardonnay, 45% Pinot Noir, and a small balance of Pinot Meunier — a Champagne-classic blend from a site Pirie selected using climate modelling he presented at the International Cool Climate Symposium in Hobart in 2012. A Deluxe Vintage Rosé, built around 90% Pinot Noir from the property's best blocks, is also produced. The Alto range adds still wines — Pinot Gris from clone D1V7 and Pinot Noir from Burgundy clones 777 and 115. Production sits between 10,000 and 20,000 bottles annually, and some of the sparkling is disgorged on the property. Apogee took out Tasmanian Vineyard of the Year in 2013, just a few years after planting.

Pirie's history in Tasmanian wine runs deep. He bought land in northern Tasmania in 1973 — the site eventually became Pipers Brook Vineyard — as part of doctoral research into cool-climate viticulture completed at the University of Sydney in 1977. The Pirie sparkling label he later made there was served to Queen Elizabeth II on at least two occasions. He left Pipers Brook in 2003, and when the chance came to apply four decades of accumulated site knowledge to a fresh planting, he took it. The water catchment on the Apogee property is fenced as a nature reserve, home to a platypus family in a running brook. The wines are the reason to visit, but the rigour behind them is the reason they're worth taking seriously.
pullQuote: Pirie spent fifty years learning how to pick this exact site — the sparkling wines show it.

name: Apsley Gorge Vineyard
description: Brian Franklin runs Apsley Gorge out of an old fish factory in Bicheno. He came to Tasmania in the early 1980s when the Melbourne building industry collapsed, spent eighteen years diving for abalone, and eventually planted 5.5 hectares of Pinot Noir and one hectare of Chardonnay several kilometres inland from the coast, near the entrance to the Apsley Gorge National Park. Cool air off the Apsley River keeps the nights fresh through autumn. The vineyard is typically one of the last in Tasmania to harvest — usually the first week of May — as Franklin waits for full phenolic ripeness before picking.

Two wines are made: Pinot Noir and Chardonnay. The Chardonnay, from a single hectare of mature vines, rarely exceeds 100 dozen bottles per year. The Pinot Noir is the flagship. Fruit is hand-picked, table-sorted, and has been 100% destalked historically, though recent vintages have introduced 10–15% whole-bunch. An extended cold maceration precedes fermentation on wild yeasts. Malolactic fermentation runs indigenously as temperatures rise in spring. Franklin neither fines nor filters, does not adjust acid, and ages the wine a minimum of eighteen months in barrel at 30% new oak. Verticals show real vintage variation — the 2012 dense and chewy with liquorice and dark plum; the 2014 richer and more resonant. The style is savoury and structured, with volume but no heaviness.

Franklin's winemaking foundation is Burgundian. He has worked every vintage in France since 2000, first with Philippe Charlopin and more recently with Philippe's son Yann at Domaine Charlopin-Tissier. The lineage traces back further still — to Henri Jayer, Charlopin's own mentor. Franklin now holds close to forty vintages across both hemispheres. The vineyard is non-irrigated, hand-pruned, and kept to low yields. Production is small enough that allocation is a real constraint. Apsley Gorge does not have a cellar door experience in any conventional sense. The reason to seek it out is simple: the wines are hard to find anywhere else, and there is no other producer in Tasmania quite like it.
pullQuote: Forty vintages between Burgundy and Tasmania, and it shows in every bottle.

name: Bellebonne
description: Bellebonne's cellar door operates out of Tasmania's Piper's River corridor, one of the coldest and slowest-ripening winegrowing districts in Australia. That cold is the point. The tasting experience here is built entirely around sparkling wine — no reds, no still whites, no hedging. Every bottle poured reflects a single, deliberate commitment.

Winemaker Natalie Fryar works exclusively with traditional-method sparkling, drawing on Chardonnay and Pinot Noir grown in the Piper's River region. The range runs from the entry-level Bis non-vintage blanc and rosé at around $52, through the vintage cuvée and rosé, up to the Blanc de Blancs — the flagship. The 2019 Blanc de Blancs received 96 points from sparkling specialist Tyson Stelzer, who named it his Sparkling White of the Year for 2025, praising the wine's nine-year evolution from lemon and white peach into glacé pear, with barrel fermentation and extended lees maturation adding spice, brioche, and a texture he credited specifically to Fryar. Only 1,212 bottles were made. At the furthest end of the range sits the 2015 Late Disgorged Vintage, available in two- and three-bottle packs at $600 and $885 respectively — wines that have been resting on lees for the better part of a decade.

Fryar established Bellebonne with one focus: to make the best sparkling wine in Australia. That isn't an unusual ambition in Tasmania, but few have structured an entire operation so narrowly around it. No still wines exist as a fallback. The cellar door is open Tuesday through Sunday, 10am to 4pm. A wine club provides early access to pre-release and museum-release cuvées, including the limited Late Disgorged bottlings, along with 15–20% discounts and free shipping. For anyone seriously interested in how Tasmania's cold-climate Chardonnay develops under extended lees aging, a visit to Bellebonne is one of the most focused tastings the island offers.
pullQuote: The Blanc de Blancs shows what Piper's River Chardonnay can do with time and a steady hand.

name: Bremley Vineyard
description: Bremley sits at the northern edge of the Coal River Valley, tucked beneath Gunnings Sugarloaf in a natural amphitheater that cuts the worst of the summer north-westerlies. The effect is real and measurable: long, warm days and cold nights, the kind of diurnal range that defines what the Coal River does best. The soils here — black cracking clays shot through with dolerite — are not what you find across most of the valley floor.

The vineyard grows Chardonnay, Pinot Noir, Pinot Blanc, and Gamay across 13 hectares, with the Chardonnay block dating to the early 2000s. For years the fruit was sold to other producers, which means some of Tasmania's better-known labels have quietly drawn on this site. The decision to make single-vineyard wines under the Bremley name brought in winemakers Justin Bubb and Anna Pooley — both serious figures in Tasmanian wine — and their involvement shapes the precision of what ends up in the bottle. The style leans into the site rather than working around it: the cool nights preserve acidity, the clay holds moisture, and the wines tend to reflect that tension between ripeness and restraint.

James and Vanessa Bresnehan bought the property in 2008 and spent the better part of a decade expanding it from two hectares to thirteen before turning their attention to making wine under their own label. That sequence matters: they learned the block as growers first, which is a different kind of knowledge than most vineyard owners arrive with. The Gamay planting is an unusual call for the Coal River Valley and worth paying attention to — it is the kind of variety that either suits a site or it doesn't, and at Bremley the amphitheater's warmth gives it a reasonable argument. Visitors coming to the Coal River for the day should factor Bremley in — the combination of an unusual site and credible winemaking talent makes it one of the more interesting stops in the valley.
pullQuote: The Pooley and Bubb connection gives this fruit the treatment it probably always deserved.

name: Brinktop Vineyard
description: Brinktop sits on the rocky slopes of Killara Farm in the Coal River Valley, where Todd Goebel and Gill Christian live above the vines they tend. The cellar door is open Friday and Saturday afternoons, and tastings here are unhurried — hands-on winemaking experiences are offered to those who want them. This is a working property first, a visitor destination second, and that order of priority is apparent from the moment you arrive.

The site has real viticultural logic behind it. East-north-east facing slopes limit harsh afternoon sun exposure, dolerite soils deliver mineral depth, and sea breezes from Frederick Henry Bay help preserve aromatics through the growing season. The wines are shaped by these conditions — cool-climate in character, with a stated focus on purity and texture over weight. Brinktop does not publicise critical scores or trophy cabinets, which puts the emphasis squarely on what is in the glass each vintage. The approach is minimal-intervention, single-site, and explicitly seasonal: Goebel and Christian are transparent that each vintage will differ as weather dictates, and they treat that variability as the point rather than a problem to be managed.

Goebel and Christian began growing grapes in the Coal River Valley in 1999, accumulating more than fifteen years of site knowledge before relocating in 2017 to establish the current vineyard on Killara Farm. The move was significant — this is now where they live, farm, and make wine, collapsing any distance between the producer and the place. The farm itself carries more than a century of agricultural history, previously worked by the Iles family for dairy, pigs, and sheep across several generations. That continuity is not incidental to how Brinktop presents itself. For visitors who want to stay, a two-bedroom self-contained cottage sits just above the vines. The combination of on-site accommodation and a working winery run by its founders makes this a rare opportunity to spend real time inside a Tasmanian wine operation rather than passing through it.
pullQuote: Single-site Coal River Pinot with genuine mineral edge — this is what the valley can do.

name: Broad Acres Vineyard
description: Before you reach the cellar door at Broad Acres, you pass through the vineyard itself — five varieties planted on a coastal strip between Ulverstone and Devonport, with Bass Strait visible to the north and the Tasmanian highlands rising to the south. The on-site cafe, Windows on Westella, sits within the vineyard grounds and runs a full table-service menu seven days a week. It is not a tasting room with a cheese board. It is a working cafe where the wine is the point, but not the only one.

Broad Acres grows Pinot Noir, Pinot Gris, Chardonnay, Sauvignon Blanc, and Riesling, and their track record at competitive shows is built almost entirely on the Pinot Noir. The 2022 vintage took gold at the Sydney Royal Wine Show; the 2023 took silver; the 2019, 2020, and 2021 vintages each collected bronze across Sydney Royal, Royal Hobart, and the Australian and National Cool Climate Wine Shows. The Pinot Gris and Sauvignon Blanc have also placed at both Sydney Royal and the National Cool Climate Wine Show. Every bunch is hand-picked and delivered to a local winery for production — a small-operation approach that keeps volumes tight and quality accountable. The portfolio also includes a Sparkling Rosé and a Blanc de Blancs.

The property's roots go back to 1983, when Mike and Gaynor Broadby established their approach to growing on this site. The vineyard as it now operates was formally established in 2016, carrying forward that original emphasis on hands-on work and close attention through each season. It is a family-scaled operation on the Cradle Coast — a part of Tasmania that doesn't always get the attention of the Tamar Valley or Coal River — and the consistent medal count across eight consecutive vintages suggests that gap in attention is the visitor's advantage. Come for the Pinot Noir, but factor in enough time to sit down properly at Windows on Westella.
pullQuote: Seven consecutive vintages of medal-winning Pinot Noir from a part of Tasmania most visitors overlook.

name: Broad Arrow Wines
description: The cellar door at Broad Acres sits between Ulverstone and Devonport on the Cradle Coast, with Bass Strait close enough that the salt air is part of the proposition. The onsite cafe, Windows on Westella, runs full table service seven days a week — so a tasting here tends to stretch into lunch, anchored by pickled onion dishes and Tasmanian platters that are apparently a point of local pride.

The vineyard grows five varieties: Pinot Noir, Sauvignon Blanc, Chardonnay, Pinot Gris, and Riesling. Pinot Noir is where the track record is clearest — consecutive vintages from 2019 through 2024 have picked up medals at both the Sydney Royal Wine Show and the Royal Hobart Wine Show, with the 2022 taking gold at Sydney Royal in 2024. The approach is hands-on from the start: every bunch is hand-picked, with fruit delivered promptly to the winery. The cool maritime climate at this latitude does a lot of the work, keeping acidity fresh and ripening measured. The sparkling range — a Rosé and a Blanc de Blancs — rounds out the lineup.

The property carries older roots than the 2016 vineyard establishment date suggests. The standards of care go back to 1983, when Mike and Gaynor Broadby set the original benchmark on this land. The current vineyard continues under that same ethos, now with a wine range that has earned consistent recognition at the national level. For visitors travelling between Devonport and the northwest, Broad Acres offers something more considered than a roadside stop — the combination of a working vineyard, a proper cafe, and a Pinot program with real form makes it worth building around.
pullQuote: The Pinot Noir has outperformed its coastal address more than once now.

name: Bundaleera Vineyard
description: Bundaleera sits on two hectares on the southern escarpment of the North Esk River, about fifteen minutes south of Launceston in Relbia. The cellar door opens most Sundays, and the pace there reflects the scale of the operation — unhurried, conversational, with a larder stocked for impromptu picnics. Two farm-stay cottages on the property mean visitors can stay overnight rather than rush back to the city.

The vineyard grows Pinot Noir, Chardonnay, Riesling, Rosé, and Sparkling — a range that tracks closely with what the Tamar Valley does well. At two hectares, production is small and the winemaking is locally handled, though the website offers little technical detail about approach or style. What the range does suggest is a producer focused on variety-driven Tasmanian standards rather than experimental outliers. For those wanting specifics on vintage or method, a visit and a conversation at the cellar door will likely yield more than any written source.

The name Bundaleera is drawn from an Aboriginal word meaning 'among the trees', and the property carries that character — wooded, quiet, away from the main tourist corridors of the Tamar Valley. It is family-run, and the operation reflects that: cellar door tastings, wine packages for weddings and events, grazing boxes assembled from local cheese and deli meats. The vineyard has recently added live music events to its program, hosting alt-country and Americana afternoons on the property. It is not a destination built around critical acclaim or winemaking celebrity. What it offers is a specific and unhurried version of Tasmanian wine country that is harder to find as the region grows more polished.
pullQuote: Small-scale Tamar Valley fruit, best understood by showing up on a Sunday.

name: Charles Reuben Estate
description: Charles Reuben Estate sits at 777 Middle Tea Tree Road, outside Hobart, with a self-contained cottage set directly among the vines. The cellar door keeps focused hours — four days a week, Saturday running longest — and for those who can't make the drive, Stall 218 at Salamanca Market runs tastings every Saturday. It is a small operation that has arranged itself around genuine access rather than spectacle.

The estate is best known for its fortified Pinot Noir, which stands apart from the dry table wines that dominate Tasmanian production. Chardonnay is also a house strength. In 2019, the property added a distillery — the claim is that it produces the first Tasmanian brandies of note — bringing the spirits range alongside the wine program. Whether that claim holds up to scrutiny across the broader Australian brandy landscape is a fair question, but the commitment to distillation as a serious pursuit rather than a novelty is evident in how prominently it features in the estate's identity. The winery describes its approach as paddock-to-glass, with environmental practice woven into production at the vineyard level.

The vineyard was established in 1989, giving it over three decades in the ground before the Volkmann family purchased the business in 2013. That transition brought new direction without erasing the site's history — the vines have age, and the fortified program in particular reflects what older Tasmanian fruit can deliver when the winemaking works with concentration rather than against it. The accommodation cottage adds a reason to stay overnight rather than pass through, and the combination of wine, spirits, and a working distillery makes Charles Reuben Estate one of the more complete single-property experiences in the Coal River Valley. Come for the fortified Pinot; leave with a bottle of brandy you didn't expect to buy.
pullQuote: The fortified Pinot Noir alone justifies the detour from Hobart.

name: Chartley Estate
description: Chartley Estate sits on the West Tamar, where the vineyard looks out over the river and the water is never far from mind. That proximity isn't incidental — it's the point. Owners Loraine and Peter built this place after years working the Tasmanian coast as abalone divers, and that background gives the estate an identity you won't find rehearsed at most cellar doors. The same instinct for selection they applied underwater — reading conditions, knowing what to take and what to leave — transferred directly to how they approach viticulture.

The wines are award-winning and sit at the premium end of what the West Tamar produces. Without specific tasting notes on record here, what the estate's approach suggests is careful, hands-on viticulture with hand-harvested fruit sorted rigorously before reaching the winery. The West Tamar's cool climate is well suited to Pinot Noir and Chardonnay, the varieties that define serious Tasmanian wine, and the site's proximity to the river moderates temperature in ways that can extend hang time and build complexity. The range is small by design, and the wines are stocked selectively rather than distributed broadly.

Peter and Loraine came to winemaking from the sea, not from viticulture school or an inherited property. That unconventional path is their most distinctive credential. The discipline required to free-dive for abalone commercially — patience, physical commitment, an eye for quality under pressure — shaped how they built the vineyard from the ground up. It's a small operation, and it stays that way deliberately. Visitors who make the drive to the West Tamar find a property that doesn't perform for crowds. The cellar door experience is straightforward and personal, which in Tasmania's increasingly busy wine tourism circuit is its own kind of draw.
pullQuote: The West Tamar has plenty of scenery; Chartley has an actual story behind the wine.

name: Chatto Wines
description: Chatto Wines operates out of the Huon Valley on a scale that is deliberately, almost stubbornly small. The estate vineyard, Isle, yields roughly one kilogram of fruit per vine. That constraint is the whole point — it shapes every decision made here, from clone selection to the allocation waitlist that new customers join and wait their turn on, in chronological order.

The focus is Pinot Noir, full stop. Isle and Intrigue are the two estate wines, both sourced from a single vineyard planted with eight clones; Isle is a selection of three or four of them depending on the season, while Intrigue uses all eight. The 2020 Isle drew 96 points from Gary Walsh, who described it as 'a wine of gravitas and purpose.' Beyond the estate, Jim Chatto sources from growers across Tasmania — the Seven Inch from a cool Huon Valley site, the Killara Farm from the sunnier Coal River Valley — and has also made small quantities of premier cru Burgundy, including Aux Vergelesses and Les Lavières, since 2019. Winemaking is restrained but not hands-off: whole-bunch fermentation features where the fruit warrants it, and the stated aim is wines of purity, line, and honest acidity rather than weight.

Jim Chatto is a name that carries real weight in Australian Pinot — he spent years as chief winemaker at Bay of Fires before starting this project in the Huon Valley. The tiny production and standing-order model are not affectations; they reflect the reality of what a genuinely small vineyard can produce in a climate that does not always cooperate. The regional and Burgundy ranges sit alongside the estate wines without diluting the focus — if anything, they reflect a winemaker comfortable enough in his own abilities to look outward. For visitors, the wines are not poured at a cellar door in any conventional sense; the relationship here is built through allocation lists and direct orders, which makes tracking down a bottle part of the experience itself.
pullQuote: The Isle is the kind of Tasmanian Pinot that makes you stop mid-glass and reconsider what you thought you knew.

name: Dawson James Wines
description: Dawson James is not a winery you visit so much as a winery you seek out. There is no cellar door in the conventional sense — just two winemakers, a long-term arrangement with the Ellis family at Meadowbank vineyard in the Derwent Valley, and wines that have been building a quiet reputation since the first vintage fifteen years ago.

The focus is singular: Chardonnay and Pinot Noir, both single-vineyard, both drawn from vines now over thirty years old on the Meadowbank site roughly sixty kilometres northwest of Hobart. The soils here are lean, and the climate is cool and dry enough to push ripening late, concentrating flavour without inflating alcohol. Peter Dawson and Tim James take a minimal-intervention approach — their stated aim is to pick at natural flavour and acid balance, then get out of the way. The wines are not made to a house style so much as to a place. James Halliday described them in 2012 as 'wine made for love, not profit,' which is either high praise or a caution about availability, depending on your perspective.

Peter Dawson and Tim James came to this project with a combined eighty-plus vintages of experience and a specific conviction: that Tasmania will eventually produce Australia's finest Chardonnay and Pinot Noir, and that Meadowbank is one of the sites that will prove it. That bet is not a new one — they have been making the case bottle by bottle since the project began — and the age of the vines now gives the wines a depth that younger Tasmanian sites are still working toward. For a visitor, the appeal is less about a destination experience and more about tracking down the wine itself. Start there.
pullQuote: The Meadowbank vines are old enough now that the wines are starting to say something definitive.

name: Domaine Dawnelle
description: Domaine Dawnelle sits on Granton Heights, a site Michael O'Brien found within two years of relocating his family to Tasmania specifically to grow Pinot Noir. That deliberateness matters. He arrived with requirements already drawn up, looked until the land matched them, and stopped there.

The focus at Domaine Dawnelle is tight: Pinot Noir and Chardonnay, the two varieties the site was chosen to grow. O'Brien trained in viticulture and winemaking and spent years working across Australian and international regions before settling here, and that experience shapes an approach that keeps the winemaking subordinate to the fruit. The wines are not widely distributed, which means what reaches the market tends to reflect considered decisions about what is actually ready to release. Without publicly available tasting notes or scoring records to draw from, the wines are best understood through the clarity of their intent — single-site Tasmanian Pinot and Chardonnay from a location chosen before the vines were ever planted.

The winery is named for Michael's late grandmother and her rice farm in country New South Wales, where he grew up. His grandfather worked in a distillery, and O'Brien traces his relationship with fermentation and land back to those years in the Riverina. The move to Tasmania in 2008 was not opportunistic — it was the conclusion of a long working life spent identifying where he actually wanted to make wine. Granton Heights, on the Derwent Valley's eastern slopes, gave him the answer. For visitors, this is a small producer operating with a specific purpose on a site that was chosen rather than inherited, and that distinction comes through in the glass.
pullQuote: A Derwent Valley Pinot from someone who moved here specifically to grow it — that focus shows.

name: Eastford Creek Vineyard
description: The cellar door at Eastford Creek sits inside a converted Granary and Pumpkin Shed — farm buildings that were here long before the vines were. That detail matters. It gives the place a material honesty that purpose-built cellar doors rarely have, and it means the visit feels grounded in the actual history of the land rather than a curated version of it.

The vineyard was only planted in 2018, which makes this operation young even by Tasmanian standards. Winemaker Andrew Gaman brings significant credentials to that youth — raised on a Margaret River winery, trained in viticulture, and now working as both a maker and a wine judge. The wines are produced in small batches on-site, with Gaman's involvement at every stage from vine to bottle. The grape varieties and specific labels are still building their track record, as you would expect from a vineyard less than a decade old, but the production philosophy is hands-on and exacting. The rocky outcrop site above Eastford Creek, away from the fertile flats of the North West Coast, shapes what the fruit expresses.

Rob and Sue Nichols planted their first vines on land that had spent years growing broccoli — a detail that tells you something about the pragmatic, low-pretension spirit of the place. The decision to diversify came from family persuasion and, reportedly, a bottle of wine. What followed was a full property conversion: farm buildings repurposed, an award-winning winemaker recruited, a cellar door and events operation built around Rachel and Nick Turner. The Pumpkin Shed now hosts weddings. The whole enterprise has been built by two families working in close proximity, which gives Eastford Creek a particular social texture. It is a working property that has added winemaking to its identity rather than replaced everything that came before. That continuity is worth a visit in itself.
pullQuote: Gaman's winemaking pedigree is real — this young vineyard is in capable hands.

name: Eversley Vines
description: Getting to Eversley Vines requires finding a secluded laneway off the Tamar Valley Wine Route, then arriving at a rocky bush block that still has no mains power or water. Matthew and Shelley Eversley-Smith built this place from scratch on a 23-acre property that was bought as a lifestyle block, not a vineyard. The north-east facing slope, warmed by dolerite and ironstone soils, changed their thinking. The surrounding bushland on three sides stays largely intact, and a small flock of sheep graze between the vines in the off-season. This is not a winery that came with infrastructure.

Eversley Vines planted 4,000 vines across 1.5 hectares in 2018, beginning from calloused cuttings propagated on-site the year before. The entire output is Pinot Noir and Chardonnay, with three clones of each variety in the ground — a deliberate choice that gives the winemaking some range within a tight footprint. Yields are still growing season by season. Organic-based fertilisers replace synthetic inputs, and herbicide use is minimal. All pruning is done by hand through winter, which Matthew and Shelley credit directly for the quality of fruit the vines produce. The wines are small-volume and tightly made, reflecting a vineyard at an early but considered stage of its development.

The name Eversley has been attached to fruit growing in Tasmania for over a century. Robert Eversley Smith ran orchards at Birch's Bay until the 1967 bushfires destroyed the property. He rebuilt under the Eversley Cherries brand at Kayena, and his son Peter became one of the state's most respected cherry growers before retiring in 2016. Shelley, Robert's granddaughter, carries the name into a fourth generation — this time with vines rather than orchard trees. Uncle Peter's knowledge of sustainable fruit growing in Tasmania passed directly into this project. For visitors, the draw is less about cellar door polish and more about seeing what serious attention to a single small site, from the ground up, actually looks like.
pullQuote: Four generations of fruit growing discipline shows up clearly in how these vines are managed.

name: Frankcomb Vineyard
description: Frankcomb Vineyard is, in fact, Frank's Cider — a ciderhouse and café on a working heritage orchard in Franklin, deep in Tasmania's Huon Valley. The trees here are old. Some were planted over 160 years ago, making this among the oldest heritage orchards in Australia. Fruit ripens later here than anywhere else in the valley, and that unhurried season is not incidental to what ends up in the glass.

Frank's produces cider rather than wine, drawing on apples and pears grown on the property across multiple generations of cultivation. Their range includes a Summer Apple Cider, Summer Pear Cider, Raspberry Pear Cider, and Cherry Pear Cider — styles that lean into the natural acidity and late-ripening character of Huon Valley fruit. The approach is orchard-led: what the trees produce in a given season shapes what gets made. The ciders are distributed nationally through Dan Murphy's and selected independents, though tasting them at the source, where the fruit was grown, is a different proposition entirely.

The Clark family has farmed this land since John Clark established the property at Woodside in 1836, making it one of the longest-running agricultural operations in the region. Frank Clark — the third generation on the farm — planted many of the trees still being harvested today. His name was given to the cider brand when Naomie Clark-Port and her husband Tony Port, the fifth generation, founded Frank's Cider Company in 2012. The Ciderhouse and Café opened in 2014, offering orchard tours alongside tastings. For visitors making their way through the Huon Valley, this is a place where the age of what you're drinking is not a marketing claim but a matter of record.
pullQuote: When the orchard is 160 years old, the cider doesn't need much explanation.

name: Glenelg Estate
description: The sandstone homestead at Glenelg Estate has stood in the Upper Derwent Valley since 1828, and it still sets the tone for everything here. The Downie family has farmed this land through six generations — merino sheep first, now pinot noir and cool-climate varieties suited to one of Tasmania's more inland growing zones. That continuity is not decorative. It shapes how the property is managed and what the land has become.

Glenelg currently operates primarily as a grape grower rather than a finished wine producer, supplying fruit to some of Tasmania's more established labels. Their own wine release is pending, described on the estate's website as coming soon. What is known is the fruit profile: the Upper Derwent Valley delivers stable summers, cold winters, and free-draining soils that tend to produce grapes with firm natural acidity and measured ripeness. Pinot noir from this sub-region typically shows restraint rather than generosity — cool-grown fruit that rewards patience in the winery. Until the estate label arrives, the wines carrying Glenelg fruit exist under other producers' names.

The Downie family's original land grant of 1,000 acres placed them among the early pastoral settlers of the Derwent Valley, and sheep still graze the property under lease. The shift toward viticulture represents a deliberate repositioning of the enterprise, not an abandonment of what came before. For visitors, there is no cellar door in the conventional sense yet — the estate is at an earlier stage than that. But the property itself, the history embedded in the homestead, and the seriousness with which the family approaches grape growing make it worth following closely. When the estate wine finally releases, it will arrive with more context than most new Tasmanian labels can claim.
pullQuote: The fruit goes into serious bottles — the estate's own label is the one to watch.

name: Grey Sands Vineyard
description: Grey Sands sits on Kerrisons Road in Glengarry, in Tasmania's upper Tamar Valley — a part of the island that tends toward cooler, slower ripening than the valley floor. The property takes its name from the grey sandy loam soils underfoot, a detail that matters here because the winemakers take it seriously enough to put it on the label.

The range includes Pinot Noir and Chardonnay, as you would expect from a serious Tasmanian producer, but Grey Sands is worth attention for its work with less common varieties. Their white blend 'Byzantine' combines Chardonnay, Viognier, and Malvasia — the last of these a Mediterranean variety rarely grown in Australia. The choice is not arbitrary: the winery traces the name and the grape back to the Eastern Roman Empire, when Malvasia vines travelled the Mediterranean coastline from port to port. Whether that history translates to the glass is a fair question, but the willingness to grow Malvasia at all signals a winemaker thinking beyond the obvious. The stated ambition — wines made to last — suggests a preference for structure over early accessibility.

Grey Sands is a small, owner-operated vineyard, the kind of Tasmanian producer that exists because someone committed to a particular piece of ground and stayed with it. The Tamar Valley has become one of the state's most established cool-climate wine corridors, and Glengarry sits comfortably within that reputation. What sets Grey Sands apart is the combination of long-term seriousness about Pinot Noir and Chardonnay alongside a genuine curiosity about what else the site can do. Visitors won't find a large cellar door operation. What they will find is direct access to wines that reflect specific choices made by the people who grew them — and the chance to ask why Malvasia ended up in northern Tasmania.
pullQuote: The Byzantine blend alone makes Grey Sands worth the detour — Malvasia in Tasmania is genuinely rare.

name: Handpicked Wines
description: Handpicked Wines operates its Tasmania cellar door with the hours of a serious hospitality venue — open seven days, from early morning to late evening — which tells you something about how the company thinks about access. This is not a weekend-only farm gate. It is a considered retail and tasting operation, one arm of a multi-region Australian wine business that has been building toward this island presence for years.

Tasmania is Handpicked's most recent vineyard acquisition, and the wines coming out of it are firmly in the cool-climate mould the island is known for: Pinot Noir and sparkling cuvée are the headline acts. The Tasmania Pinot Noir is released annually — 2021, 2022, 2023, and 2024 vintages are all currently available at $63 — and the NV Tasmania Sparkling Cuvée sits at $60. The company holds organic certifications across its portfolio and applies a minimal-intervention philosophy, aiming to let site speak rather than winemaker. Given Tasmania's natural acidity and slow ripening, that restraint suits the fruit well.

Handpicked began sourcing from premium Australian regions around the turn of the millennium, eventually anchoring its operations with the purchase of the Capella vineyard on the Mornington Peninsula — named for the star — before expanding into the Yarra Valley and Barossa. Tasmania came last, and the company frames it as part of a deliberate argument about Australian regionality: that the same producer can make genuinely distinct wines if the vineyard selection is right. Whether that argument holds is something visitors can test directly. The cellar door's long opening hours make it one of the more genuinely convenient stops in the region, and tasting across the Tasmanian releases back-to-back with a vintage or two is a reasonable way to spend an hour.
pullQuote: The Pinot Noir is clean and site-honest — what you'd want from Tasmania at this price.

name: Heemskerk Wines
description: Invercarron sits on a north-facing block above the Jordan Valley floor, high enough that you can see the sheep grazing on the flats below while standing among the vines. It is a working farm first — sheep, crops, and now grapes — and the vineyard feels like exactly that: something added with purpose to land that was already being used seriously.

Vines went in for the first time in 2017, with Pinot Gris, Chardonnay, and Pinot Noir chosen for the cool southern Tasmanian conditions. The debut release announced the project clearly: the inaugural Pinot Noir scored 93 points from respected critic Huon Hooke. For a first vintage from a new site in an unestablished sub-region, that is a result worth noting. The range remains small and focused, which at this stage of the vineyard's development is the right call.

Andrew and Karen Jones bought the property from Andrew's parents, keeping seven generations of Jones family farming in the Jordan Valley intact. Andrew came to it without any farming background — his own admission — and spent two decades running a travel agency before making the shift. The vineyard, planted in 2017 with three generations of the family helping, makes Invercarron the first in the region. That pioneering position carries real weight: there is no established playbook for the Jordan Valley, and what Invercarron learns about this site over the next decade will matter beyond their own label. The Picker's Hut accommodation adds a reason to stay longer than a single tasting, and the scale of the operation means the experience remains genuinely personal.
pullQuote: A 93-point debut Pinot Noir from an untested sub-region is not an accident.

name: Invercarron Wines
description: Invercarron sits on a north-facing block above the Jordan Valley floor, high enough that you can see the sheep paddocks and working fields below while standing among the vines. It is a functioning mixed farm first and a vineyard second — and that context matters. The winery did not arrive here looking for a lifestyle; the vines were planted because the farm needed a next chapter.

The first vines went in the ground in 2017 — Pinot Noir, Chardonnay, and Pinot Gris, all suited to the cool southern Tasmanian conditions at this elevation. The inaugural vintage made an immediate mark: the Pinot Noir scored 93 points from critic Huon Hooke, a notable result for a first release from a region with no winemaking history. The site selection involved extensive soil testing, and the north-facing aspect gives the fruit enough warmth to ripen without surrendering the acidity that defines good cool-climate wine. The range remains tight and focused, which at this stage of the operation is the right call.

The Jones family has farmed the Jordan Valley since 1818, though the land itself has changed hands and addresses across seven generations. Andrew and Karen Jones came to Invercarron after two decades running a travel agency — not a romantic escape, but a deliberate pivot toward something slower and more grounded. Andrew bought the property from his parents, Sue and Phil, in part to keep it in the family. The winery came later, driven by Andrew's instinct to find the next productive use of the land. Broadmarsh remains off the radar for most Tasmania wine visitors, which means a trip to Invercarron still carries some of the quality that disappears once a region becomes well known. The Jordan Valley is an argument waiting to be made, and Invercarron is currently the only one making it.
pullQuote: A 93-point debut Pinot Noir from a region no one had planted before — that's worth paying attention to.

name: Kinvarra Wines
description: The original 1827 homestead still stands on the Kinvarra property, looking out over vines that weren't planted until 1990. That gap — 163 years between the estate's founding and its first vintage — tells you something about the pace of this place. Situated in the upper Derwent Valley near Plenty, roughly 40 minutes from Hobart, the 740-hectare property spent most of its life as grazing land before a single hectare was hand-planted to Riesling and Pinot Noir as a kind of test. It passed.

That original home block, now over three decades old, supplies the fruit for Kinvarra's still wines — a Riesling and a Pinot Noir, both handpicked, both priced accessibly under AUD $25. The Pinot leans toward texture and length rather than weight, with oak used in support rather than as a statement. The Riesling runs citrus and green apple with a generous mid-palate. In 2009, a further 8.7 hectares of Chardonnay and Pinot Noir went in, dedicated entirely to sparkling wine production using traditional method blends of Pinot Noir, Chardonnay, and Pinot Meunier. The estate's wines have appeared in the James Halliday Australian Wine Companion, though critical coverage remains limited relative to Tasmania's more prominent producers.

The estate's modern chapter belongs to the Bevan family, who bought in partly as a lifestyle decision — four sons, a property in one of Tasmania's quieter valleys, a working vineyard. It is a small operation by most measures: under 10 hectares of vines on land that dwarfs them. But the combination of old-vine Riesling and Pinot Noir alongside a serious sparkling program gives Kinvarra a range that few estates its size can match. Visitors making the drive up the Derwent Valley get the added context of Mount Field National Park visible to the west — not decoration, but a reminder of why cool-climate viticulture in this valley works at all.
pullQuote: The still wines are honest and underpriced; the sparkling program is the real reason to pay attention.

name: La Villa Wines
description: La Villa Wines sits on land that Marcus's family has farmed for over three generations, first as an apple and pear orchard in Spreyton, near Devonport. The transition from fruit trees to vines didn't erase that agricultural history — it built on it. When you visit, you're standing on ground that has been worked continuously by the same family since well before Tasmania's wine industry existed.

The vineyard has grown from an initial 800 vines to just over nine hectares of cool-climate varieties, a scale that keeps production focused and hands-on. Tasmania's northern Midlands climate — long, cool ripening seasons and reliable maritime influence — suits varieties that need time to develop structure without losing acidity. The winery's approach reflects its origins as a hobby project taken seriously: incremental, observational, unhurried. Specific variety and vintage information is best sought directly at the cellar door, where the family can speak to each wine from the ground up.

The story behind La Villa is unusually traceable. Marcus and Gail spent years in Emilia-Romagna in the early 1980s during Marcus's professional cycling career, and returned to Italy 26 years later to reconnect with the friends they had made there — people who had built their lives around food, wine, and land. Picking grapes at the Volpe winery with their friend Marco was enough. They came home and planted vines on the family orchard. Their daughter Effie has since returned from Melbourne, where she practiced law and ran a florist, and is now working across the vineyard, cellar door, and new projects still taking shape. The cellar door is open Wednesday through Sunday, October to May. For a winery still finding its full form, that limited season makes a visit feel less like a transaction and more like catching something in progress.
pullQuote: Three generations of farming the same land gives these wines a rootedness most new Tasmanian producers simply can't fake.

name: Leven Valley Vineyard
description: Leven Valley Vineyard sits on a north-facing slope above Gunns Plains, about twenty minutes south of Ulverstone, and its position matters more than most. The deep red and black clay loams over limestone give the site a terroir that is genuinely distinct in Tasmanian terms. The cellar door is unhurried — visitors are welcome to bring their own food and eat in the garden, which is either a charming detail or a reason to plan ahead, depending on how you like to travel.

The vineyard grows Chardonnay and Pinot Noir across 1.5 hectares, and the combination of latitude and soil type means these grapes ripen later than almost anywhere else in Australia. That extended hang time is the point. The wines carry cold-climate character in a way that warmer Tasmanian sites simply cannot replicate. Winemaking has historically been hands-on and local, with vintages hand-picked by volunteers and friends — an approach that keeps quantities small and quality a direct function of care taken in the vineyard. The label has accumulated multiple awards at the Hobart Wine Show across successive vintages.

Chardonnay and Pinot Noir vines were first planted here in 1997 by Steve and Diana Usher, with the first commercial vintage following in 2001 under the Moonrakers label. The name changed to Leven Valley Vineyard in 2003, and the property has since passed through two further owners — Wendy and Mike Dontschuk from 2012, and Terri and Trenton Seager from early 2022. Each transition has preserved the same small-scale, estate-focused philosophy rather than scaling up. For visitors, that continuity means the cellar door still reflects the vineyard directly behind it. The drive out to Gunns Plains is reason enough, but the wines justify the detour on their own terms.
pullQuote: Tasmania's latest-ripening site produces Pinot Noir with a structural tension that warmer vintages here simply cannot fake.

name: Lost Farm Wines
description: Lost Farm Wines sits inside a golf clubhouse — and that is not a caveat, it is the point. The restaurant and bar at Barnbougle's Lost Farm course, on Tasmania's northeast coast near Bridport, is where golfers end their rounds and where everyone else comes to watch them finish. The 18th and 18a holes funnel players directly toward the bar. The setting is coastal and windswept, the dunes genuine, the light off Bass Strait in the late afternoon worth staying for.

The wines here are drawn from Tasmania's cool-climate producers and reflect the island's strengths: Pinot Noir, Chardonnay, and sparkling wines made from Chardonnay and Pinot Noir. The list leans local and deliberately so, pairing the region's high-acid, fine-boned styles with the kind of post-round hunger that makes a good glass of Tasmanian Chardonnay taste better than it might anywhere else. The program is curated rather than produced on-site — Lost Farm does not operate its own vineyard — which means the quality depends on selection. By most accounts, that selection is taken seriously.

Barnbougle itself opened in 2004 with The Dunes course, designed by Tom Doak and Mike Clayton on what had been a coastal potato farm. Lost Farm followed in December 2010, built by Coore and Crenshaw on the steeper dunes across the river. It ranked second in Australia within weeks of opening and entered the world top 100 within eight months. The wine and dining offer grew alongside the golf reputation, giving the property a reason to visit for those who have never swung a club. The honest draw here is the combination — a serious lunch, a Tasmanian Pinot, and a view of golfers fighting the wind on the final holes — and few places in the country offer that particular combination as well as this one.
pullQuote: The Tasmanian Pinot list alone justifies the drive to Bridport.

name: Lowestoft Estate
description: The Lowestoft homestead sits above the Derwent River looking out over Lowestoft Bay, a mid-nineteenth century sandstone property that has been producing fruit from the same three hectares of Pinot Noir for nearly four decades. The vines were planted in 1986 from Moorilla cuttings at the direct request of Claudio Alcorso, the man who effectively restarted Tasmanian winemaking. That lineage runs through everything here.

Lowestoft's focus is Pinot Noir, grown on those original three hectares near Hobart. Before the Fogarty family acquired the estate in 2019, the fruit had been sold to Fred Peacock for his Bream Creek Reserve and to Treasury Wine Estates — grapes with a documented history of ending up in bottles that mattered. The current winemaking approach leans into the cool Derwent Valley conditions, where the proximity to the river and the site's modest scale work against excess. The Halliday Wine Companion called Lowestoft Tasmania's most exciting new label, a pointed compliment given the strength of the region's established names.

The Fogarty family connection to Tasmania stretches back to the mid-1800s, when the first Irish-born Fogarty arrived on the island. Peter Fogarty, founder of the Fogarty Wine Group in Western Australia, purchased Lowestoft in 2019 alongside two additional vineyards, Strelley Farm and Gilling Brook. The property's history runs even deeper than the family's — Thomas Lowes planted vines on this land before 1848 and won first prize at the Hobart Horticultural Society Show that year, making this one of the oldest viticultural sites in the south of the state. A visit here puts you on ground that has been producing wine, in some form, longer than almost anywhere else in Tasmania.
pullQuote: The vineyard's history is doing real work in the glass — this isn't a vanity project.

name: Mapleton Vineyards
description: Mapleton Vineyards is three properties operating as one — Mapleton, Woodlands, and Lynwood — spread across the Coal River Valley near Tea Tree, where the Laing family has spent the better part of a decade piecing together a vineyard from land that, in places, had been left to go quiet for years. The cellar door is open Thursdays only, which tells you something about the scale of the operation and the seriousness of the people running it.

The range covers Pinot Noir, Chardonnay, Riesling, and a small amount of Cabernet, drawn from vines of varying ages across the three blocks. The oldest date to 1992 on the Woodlands parcel — heritage material that required years of remediation before it produced reliably again. The 2018 vintage was the first to show what the Lynwood block could do: it yielded a Riesling that won both a gold medal and the Trophy for Best 2018 Vintage Wine at the Tasmanian Wine Show. That single result from a vineyard only recently brought back into production is worth paying attention to.

Paul Laing purchased the original Mapleton property in 2004, but the decision to plant vines didn't come until 2014. What followed moved quickly: a neighbouring property with established vines came up for sale, then another. Denis and Kathryn, Paul's parents, now live on Lynwood, which gives the whole enterprise a particular coherence — this is genuinely family-farmed land, not a lifestyle investment dressed up as one. The Woodlands vines, once neglected to the point of near-abandonment, now produce Pinot Noir and Chardonnay alongside their small Cabernet planting. For visitors, the Thursday-only opening is a constraint worth planning around. The combination of old-vine material, a family still actively rebuilding what they acquired, and a Riesling that already has a trophy to its name makes the detour a reasonable one.
pullQuote: That 2018 Riesling from a freshly rehabilitated vineyard should not have been that good.

name: Meadowbank Wines
description: Meadowbank sits in the Upper Derwent Valley, inland enough that the cooling sea breezes that define so much of Tasmanian viticulture barely register here. What does register are the temperature swings — warm days, genuinely cold nights — and over nearly five decades, that diurnal rhythm has shaped what this vineyard can do. The oldest vines on the property date to 1974. They have had time to find their argument.

Winemaker Peter Dredge picks by taste alone, a discipline that shows in wines that feel earned rather than engineered. The Chardonnay, drawn from P58 clone vines planted in 1987, has scored 95 points from Halliday for three consecutive years — pure, finely structured, with the kind of acid that rewards patience. The Riesling runs in a Germanic register and competes with anything else on the island. The Syrah is deliberately named as such: the 2023 won Trophy for Best Shiraz at the Tasmanian Wine Show, but the style points firmly toward the Northern Rhône rather than any warmer Australian tradition. And the 2020 Gamay took the Chairman's Award at the Tasmanian Wine Show — best wine overall. A 2016 planting of Gamay, Syrah, Pinot Noir, and Chardonnay brought the estate to 52 hectares, with a further 14 hectares added in November 2024, including the property's first Chenin Blanc.

Gerald Ellis bought the property in 1976, when growing grapes in southern Tasmania was widely considered a bad idea. He had been a sheep farmer. The land had been used as one. The family's Tasmanian roots go back to 1827 and a convict ancestor named William Ellis — a fact that gives Gerald's original defiance some historical texture. Four major expansions followed that first planting, and a partnership with Dredge has sharpened the winemaking without altering what the place fundamentally is. The 2024 Pinot Noir winning both the Jimmy Watson Memorial Trophy and the James Halliday Trophy at the 2025 Melbourne Royal Wine Awards confirmed what the valley's best vintages had been suggesting for years. Come for the Chardonnay and the Riesling; stay for what the Pinot Noir is becoming.
pullQuote: The Chardonnay and Riesling alone justify the drive inland.

name: Merriworth Wines
description: Merriworth sits in the Coal River Valley, one of Tasmania's driest and sunniest sub-regions, where the diurnal temperature swings are wide enough to preserve acidity in fruit that ripens fully. Mark and Kirralee established the property in 2017, which makes this one of the younger estates in a valley that has been drawing serious attention since the 1990s. What they are building here is still taking shape — and that, for a certain kind of wine traveller, is precisely the point.

The Coal River Valley's signature strengths run toward Pinot Noir and Chardonnay, and Merriworth's stated focus on depth and complexity over approachable early-drinking styles signals an intent to work with those varieties seriously rather than commercially. The website offers limited technical detail about individual wines, so it is difficult to point to specific bottlings or scores at this stage. What the site does convey is a commitment to site expression over formula — cool ferments, restrained intervention, and fruit grown on a property the owners know intimately because they planted it themselves.

Mark and Kirralee relocated to the Coal River Valley specifically for this project, which separates Merriworth from the inherited or opportunistic vineyard stories that are common in newer wine regions. Coming in as relative newcomers in 2017, they have had to learn the site through its vintages rather than through someone else's notes. That kind of ground-up understanding tends to show in the wine eventually, even if it takes time to arrive. For visitors, Merriworth represents a chance to taste a young estate mid-development — before the story is finished and while the winemakers are still asking questions the vines haven't fully answered yet.
pullQuote: Young vines, serious intent — the Coal River Valley has room for what this could become.

name: Richmond Park Estate
description: Richmond Park Estate sits on the Coal River, 25 kilometres from Hobart, on a thousand hectares that have been running merino sheep since long before anyone planted a single vine. The Eddington family bought the property in 1986. Four generations have known this land. The cellar door is still coming — but the winemaking is already underway, and the logic behind it is deliberate rather than opportunistic.

First vines went in during spring 2020, which makes Richmond Park Estate one of the newer entrants in the Coal River Valley. The plan was sequenced carefully: Pinot Gris and Riesling were targeted for release from 2023, with Pinot Noir, Chardonnay, and sparkling wines to follow in 2024. These are the right varieties for this site — the Coal River Valley runs dry and warm by Tasmanian standards, its dolerite soils holding heat without sacrificing the acidity that defines the region. Without a significant competition record yet established, the wines are still finding their public footing, but the variety selection and site logic are sound.

What sets Richmond Park Estate apart from nearly every other producer in Tasmania is that wine is genuinely a secondary enterprise here, not a lifestyle pivot. The property runs 3,000 merino ewes and is the driving force behind a 20-year wetlands restoration project at the junction of the Coal River and Pitt Water-Orielton Lagoon — an Australian Government-funded effort covering 65 hectares. The wool has been winning agricultural show awards since the early 1980s. The Eddingtons came to viticulture through a ten-year sustainability plan, not the other way around. For a visitor, that context changes the experience: this is a working farm that happens to be making wine, and the seriousness of the broader enterprise lends the vineyard a credibility that newer operations often lack.
pullQuote: A sheep station with a ten-year viticultural plan is not something you encounter every day.

name: Roslyn 1823
description: The Georgian homestead at Roslyn 1823 was built from bricks made and fired on-site by convicts — the same bricks that went into the construction of nearby Richmond. That history is not decorative background. It is structural, literally embedded in the walls of one of Australia's oldest cellar doors, on a 30-hectare farm along White Kangaroo Road in Campania's Coal River Valley.

Owner Andrew Palmer holds a permaculture teaching degree and has spent more than a decade applying permaculture ethics and Natural Sequence Farming techniques across the vineyard. Chickens and ducks manage pests. Sheep and alpacas handle weeds. Irrigation is kept to a minimum. The four varieties — Pinot Noir, Riesling, Chardonnay, and Pinot Gris — are hand-picked and made by Robbie Drew, a Tea Tree winemaker with 30 years of experience. The 2021 Pinot Noir, which spent nine months in French oak, was named Wine of the Week by the Australian Good Food Guide and drew notice from independent reviewers for its black cherry, graphite, and thyme character with slick, well-integrated tannins. Their Iced Riesling, a dessert wine carrying honey and citrus blossom, is less widely discussed but worth seeking out.

The estate was founded in 1823, making it among the earliest established properties in Tasmania. It is heritage-listed, and the convict-brick tower that rises from the main homestead remains one of the more visually arresting structures in the Coal River Valley. Palmer describes their philosophy as 'beyond organic' — a phrase that points to the gap between certification and practice. For a visitor, that gap is visible: the farm operates as an integrated system, not a vineyard with decorative animals. The cellar door sits inside a building that predates most Australian wine culture by decades. That combination — genuine agricultural seriousness inside a structure of genuine historical weight — is not something you find at many stops in Tasmania.
pullQuote: The 2021 Pinot Noir shows what Coal River can do when someone actually farms the site.

name: Sailor Seeks Horse
description: The vineyard at Sailor Seeks Horse was abandoned. Half the vines were dead when Gilli and Paul found it in the Huon Valley — blackberry above head height, roughly a hundred trees scattered through the rows, a NE-facing slope that nobody had bothered to finish. They bothered.

The focus here is Pinot Noir and Chardonnay, which was always the plan — the pair spent years working vintages in the Languedoc, Oregon, New Zealand, and Margaret River before landing in Tasmania with that explicit goal. The Huon Valley site, planted in 2005 across six Pinot clones, gives them the raw material: sheltered from the south-westerly, with the kind of slow, cool ripening that the variety rewards. The wines tend toward restraint rather than extraction — shaped by a site that has to work hard and produces fruit that reflects it.

The name is a rough anagram — a piece of wordplay that suits a winery built on a left-field decision. In 2005, at the London International Wine Fair, the wine writer Robert Joseph told them plainly not to do it. They studied winemaking and viticulture in Margaret River anyway, completed degrees, worked harvests across three countries, then moved to Tasmania to plant a flag in one of the Huon Valley's underexplored microclimates. The valley had form — Elsewhere and Home Hill had already put it on the map — but Sailor Seeks Horse went further south and further into the specifics of the site. The winery is small, the output is limited, and the wines are the product of people who chose this with full knowledge of the odds. That backstory is not incidental to what's in the glass.
pullQuote: The Huon Valley Pinots earn their cool-climate credentials without making a fuss about it.

name: Sisu Wines
description: Sisu Wines sits on a 95-hectare farm at 1479 Colebrook Road, Campania, in the Coal River Valley — though only 25 of those hectares are planted to vine. The rest is deliberately left open: to sheep, to native plantings, to the land doing what it does without prompting. That proportion is not an accident. It reflects a decision, made early, about what kind of farm this should be.

Jake Sheedy farms the vineyard sustainably — certified, not just claimed — and works with minimal intervention from vine to bottle. The Coal River Valley's cool temperatures and nutrient-dense soils suit wines that are structured and precise rather than broad or generous. The specific varieties Sisu produces are not widely publicised, but the valley's strengths point clearly toward cool-climate whites and Pinot Noir. What Sheedy has stated plainly is his preference for fruit that is bright, expressive, and finely structured — and a winemaking hand that shapes without dominating. These are wines made by someone who treats restraint as a discipline, not a style.

Sisu is a young operation, but its commitments are long-term ones. Two hundred and twenty sheep work the vineyard rows as a living alternative to herbicide and machinery. In partnership with Landcare Tasmania, the property has planted 1,300 native stems — prickly box, white gum, black wattle, she-oaks — as the start of a plan to rewild 10% of the land within five years. Land for Wildlife consults on the existing ecology and on what species the property could support. The name Sisu is a Finnish word carrying the sense of grit and quiet determination, and it fits. The cellar door is open Thursday through Monday. It is worth the drive out to Campania to see what farming with actual consequences looks like.
pullQuote: Coal River cool-climate discipline, from someone who clearly means it.

name: sixfriends Vineyard
description: sixfriends Vineyard sits on a sheltered hillside at Burnside, Orielton, on the eastern edge of the Coal River Valley — a site chosen not by a corporation or a developer, but by six friends over dinner in 2002. That origin still shapes what the place feels like. This is a small operation with a specific social history attached to it, and that is evident in everything from the scale of production to the care taken with individual vintages.

The vineyard produces a single-block Pinot Noir, hand-picked and made by Alain Rousseau at Frogmore Creek — one of the Coal River Valley's more experienced winemaking hands. Vines were planted progressively between 2003 and 2008, so the oldest blocks are now approaching genuine maturity. The 2014 vintage stands out in the winery's history: a wine named in memory of Penny, one of the founding six who died that year. By all accounts it was also one of their best. Low-volume, single-site Pinot Noir from this part of Tasmania tends toward precision over power, and sixfriends is consistent with that character.

The founding group — Ian, Brigid, Dave, Michele, Tim, and Penny — planted the first vines in 2003 with no prior commercial winemaking background, relying on outside advice and, apparently, considerable patience. Over two decades, those vines have matured into something worth taking seriously. The winery doesn't have a cellar door in the conventional sense, which means visitors need to engage with it differently — through their wine stockists or direct contact. But for anyone interested in what a genuinely small, friendship-founded Tasmanian Pinot Noir project looks like at the twenty-year mark, sixfriends is a worthwhile stop.
pullQuote: Twenty-year-old vines and a founding story that actually matters — this is Coal River Pinot with real context.

name: Stargazer Wine
description: Stargazer is open four days a week — Friday through Monday — which tells you something about its scale and its priorities. This is not a high-volume operation built around coach tours and corporate tastings. The cellar door is a place to sit with a glass and give it proper attention, which is exactly what the wines ask of you.

The focus is on Riesling, Chardonnay, and Pinot Noir — the three varieties that define serious Tasmanian winemaking, and the three that Stargazer handles with notable restraint. The 2024 Palisander Riesling and 2023 Chardonnay are both allocated at six bottles per customer, which suggests demand that the production volumes cannot meet. That kind of quiet scarcity is usually earned. The winemaking approach leans toward precision over intervention — cool-climate fruit handled carefully enough that the site does most of the talking.

Stargazer was founded by Samantha Connew, a New Zealander who trained across multiple continents before settling in the Coal River Valley. The winery's name references Abel Tasman, the Dutch navigator who became the first European to sight both Tasmania and New Zealand's South Island — a figure with obvious personal resonance for Connew, who crossed a similar stretch of water to make her home here. She has spoken about the influence of a high school teacher who told her always to look up, a habit that seems to have shaped both the winery's name and its ethos. Connew's background — serious, international, technically rigorous — shows in wines that avoid both the rustic and the overworked. If you are making a dedicated trip to the Coal River Valley, Stargazer is one of the stops that justifies the drive.
pullQuote: Connew's Riesling reminds you that Tasmania's cool south can do something Europe cannot quite replicate.

name: Uplands Vineyard
description: The homestead at Uplands was built around 1823 — before Melbourne existed — and that fact is not incidental. It sets the tone for everything here. The property in Cambridge, just outside Hobart in the Coal River Valley, carries two centuries of accumulated use: convict history, sheep runs, orchards, grain, and now vines. Only four families have owned it in that time. That continuity is visible in the buildings and in the way the land sits.

Uplands grows Chardonnay and Pinot Noir, with the first Chardonnay vines planted in 1998 and further plantings of both varieties added in December 2020. The grapes are processed by Tasmanian Vintners, which handles winemaking for a number of smaller Coal River producers. The standout wines are the sparkling: the 2011 Pinot Noir/Chardonnay and the 2018 and 2021 Blanc de Blancs took out Sparkling Wine of Provenance at the 2025 Royal Hobart Wine Show — a meaningful result in a region that treats its sparkling program seriously. The Coal River Valley's dry summers and cool autumns give the Chardonnay slow, even ripening and the kind of natural acidity that holds up well in traditional-method sparkling wine.

John Petchey arrived in Van Diemen's Land in 1812, transported for receiving stolen goods, and by 1825 had built a substantial house, accumulated over a thousand acres, and kept ten convict servants. He drowned in the Hobart Regatta in 1850. The second owner, William Farquhar McKay, was transported at fourteen for stealing two watches, sentenced to life, and ended up buying the property for £2,250 in 1848. The land has since passed to the McKay family descendants and then to Michael and Debbie, who opened the vineyard to visitors starting with the 2020 Southern Open Vineyards Weekend. The history here isn't atmosphere — it's the actual record of the place, and it gives a visit to Uplands a weight that newer operations simply can't manufacture.
pullQuote: The Blanc de Blancs is the quiet achiever — Coal River acid structure at its most precise.

name: Utzinger Wines
description: Utzinger sits on the western edge of the Tamar Valley, where ferrous topsoil loaded with dolerite pebbles gives way to a heavy clay subsoil beneath. The vineyard is planted at 6,500 vines per hectare — a high density that pushes roots to compete and dig deep. It is a working farm in the most literal sense, with native grass corridors, planted understory, and a deliberate effort to keep the biosystem intact rather than managed into submission.

The vineyard, established in 2018, focuses on Pinot Noir and Chardonnay — planted across five and six clones respectively — alongside Sauvignon Blanc and Syrah. That breadth of clonal selection points to a serious interest in site expression rather than a single house style. Utzinger holds certified organic status for both vineyard and winery. The winemaking philosophy is minimal intervention: the aim is to let the fruit speak for the particular block of dolerite-strewn ground it came from. Given the youth of the vines, these are wines still finding their register, but the raw material and the method are both pointed in the right direction.

The winery's origin is straightforward: a Swiss winemaker circled the globe looking for the right piece of ground, found it in northern Tasmania, and found a reason to stay. Utzinger opened to visitors Thursday through Saturday, 11am to 4pm. The property has set aside ten percent of its land as intact native grassland and planted a further fifteen percent with trees and groundcover — commitments that go beyond certification paperwork. For visitors, the draw is a vineyard young enough that you can watch it develop vintage by vintage, run by people who made a considered, specific choice to be exactly here.
pullQuote: The clonal diversity in that Pinot planting tells you this was never a casual project.

name: Wellington & Wolfe
description: Wellington & Wolfe is not a cellar door in the conventional sense — it is a single-minded project, built around one variety, run by one person with a clear idea of what Tasmanian Riesling can be. Hugh McCullough sources fruit from Pipers River and the Tamar Valley, works in small volumes, and makes every decision by hand. The first vintage, in 2017, involved a basket press and foot treading because the harvest — just over a tonne — was too small for the press at the winery he was renting space in. That set the tone.

Three distinct Rieslings sit under the Wellington & Wolfe label, all dry, all from northern Tasmania. The flagship is built on extended lees and skin contact, with partial fermentation in old French oak — techniques that pull texture and weight from a variety more often made for immediacy. The premium reserve, the Eylandt Riesling, adds further depth through careful basket pressing and cool fermentation. The second label, Wolfe at the Door, launched in 2020 and runs looser: micro-lot, naturally made, and variable by design. Past releases have included Pinot Meunier, Pinot Gris, a session Riesling, and a rosé. These are wines made to drink, not to keep.

McCullough came to winemaking through an unlikely route — a master's in modern history from St Andrews, then years in hospitality, then a second master's in viticulture and oenology, then vintages in Oregon, Washington, the Barossa, and finally Tasmania. The name Wellington & Wolfe comes from two generals he encountered repeatedly in his historical studies, names that surfaced in the places he lived and worked. He settled in Launceston with his winemaker partner Natalie Fryar of Bellebonne. The project's focus is genuinely narrow — 100 percent Riesling, 100 percent Tasmania — and that discipline is what makes it worth seeking out.
pullQuote: Tasmanian Riesling doesn't get more serious or more focused than this.

name: Wines By Rory
description: The vineyard at Fleurty's Lane sits on the shores of the D'Entrecasteaux Channel, forty kilometres south of Hobart, looking out toward Bruny Island. Planted in 1991 on basalt and Jurassic dolerite soils, the one-hectare block is among the most southerly planted vineyards in Australia. It gets 850 millimetres of rain a year and needs no irrigation. The channel keeps frost at bay. The vines do the rest.

Wines by Rory draws from Pinot Noir and Chardonnay primarily, with smaller plantings of Pinot Gris, Cabernet Franc, Malbec, and Merlot — an unusual mix for this latitude, and one that rewards curiosity. The winemaking is unadorned: wild fermentation, no fining, no filtration, and sulphur added at bottling only when necessary. No systemic sprays or herbicides have been used since 2004. The resulting wines carry the density and restraint that come from slow, cool ripening and soils that push vines hard. What complexity is in the glass came from the vineyard, not the cellar.

The vineyard's history runs through two generations of a family. Dirk D'meure, an academic lawyer who left Tasmania for Cambridge in 1970, returned in 2000 to take up winemaking on this same block — land near where his Dutch parents had settled in 1950. His nephew Rory Duggan arrived later, spent years working alongside Dirk before going on to train with Stefano Lubiana, then took over the site as caretaker when Dirk stepped back. In early 2020, Dirk bought back the Fleurty's Lane vineyard and the two formally joined forces. It is a small operation with a long memory, and that combination is exactly what makes the trip worth making.
pullQuote: These are wines made without a safety net — and they don't need one.

name: Winter Brook Vineyard
description: Winter Brook sits on Hjorts Road in the Loira area of the West Tamar Valley, about thirty minutes north of Launceston. The cellar door is on-site, meaning you're tasting in the same place the fruit was grown and the wine was made — a rarer thing than it should be. The slopes face north, the soils carry Permian-age geology laid down over 250 million years, and the whole operation runs to around 10,000 bottles a year. That number tells you most of what you need to know before you open the door.

The range covers the cool-climate staples — Pinot Noir, Chardonnay, Riesling, Pinot Gris, and a Fumé Blanc — but the more interesting signals come from the edges. Winter Brook grows Dornfelder and Blaufränkisch, German and Austrian red varieties that almost nobody else in Tasmania bothers with. Their signature red, Maverick, blends Dornfelder with Reserve Pinot Noir and Baco Noir, which is an unusual combination by any measure. The winemaking approach is low-intervention, low-preservative, estate-grown throughout. There is also a Mélange Blanc featuring Grüner Veltliner and a sparkling range including Blanc de Blanc and two Blanc de Noir expressions.

The vineyard was established in the late 1990s, but its current character took shape over two ownership changes. Nicole and Frank Huisman — she had worked at the European Space Agency, he as a soil engineer — bought what was then a 0.6-hectare hobby vineyard in 2010 and expanded it to 3.25 hectares, building the cellar door and introducing the European varieties. In mid-2021, Sandy and Adam Gibson took over, with Sandy acting as winemaker and vigneron. The Dornfelder and Maverick are the clearest reason to visit — they represent a genuine argument that Tasmania's red wine story is broader than Pinot Noir alone.
pullQuote: The Maverick is the kind of red that makes you reconsider what Tasmania can do.
